topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification pinged on my phone screen, a small, unwelcome interruption to the quiet hum of my afternoon. I glanced at the profile picture attached. It was him. Mark. The same greasy grin, the same slightly too-wide smile I remembered from the long-ago hallways of middle school. My thumb hovered over the 'Accept' button, then retreated. I didn't bother clicking. A strange, flat sensation settled in my chest, like a pebble dropped into still water. I scrolled through my contacts, the list blurring into meaningless names. Why would he want to connect now? I simply closed the messaging app and turned back to the textbook, the words on the page refusing to hold any real interest.

[story 2]

A chime announced a new connection request. My jaw tightened almost imperceptibly as I looked at the sender's name: Mark. I let the phone rest on the desk, the cool glass a familiar anchor. I didn't feel the usual spike of anxiety or annoyance. Instead, a kind of dull numbness washed over me, smoothing out the edges of my day. I watched the little notification bubble, acknowledging its existence without giving it any weight. I didn't even open the profile. It was just another data point in a long, unremarkable sequence of digital noise. I picked up my lukewarm coffee, the bitter taste doing nothing to stir anything within me.

[story 3]

The message appeared: "Hey." It was brief, utterly devoid of context, just a single word from the person who used to thrive on making me feel small. I stared at the screen for a full minute. My fingers twitched, ready to swipe it away, but they stayed still. There was no surge of anger, no sudden rush of defensive adrenaline. Just a quiet acknowledgment of the intrusion. I sighed, a slow, breathy sound that seemed to carry no actual feeling. I set the phone face down, the faint glow reflecting off the polished wood of the table. It was just another thing that had happened, another ripple on a surface I had long since grown accustomed to ignoring.
